---
title: "From RNNs to the Attention Mechanism"
description: Transformers part one
layout: default
date: 2026-03-05
categories: [Machine Learning, Neural Network]
bibliography: transformers.bib
format:
  html:
    css: ../../styles.css
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 3
    fig-cap-location: top
    math:
      method: mathjax
---


This post roughly follows the lecture @StanfordAttention.

# Encoder-Decoder RNNs
Consider the problem of sequence-to-sequence modeling. A canonical example is machine translation:
the input sequence  $x_1, \dots, x_N$ is a chunk of text in one language, and the goal is to produce 
a new sequence $y_1, \dots, y_M$ representing the same text in some other language. 
Translating word-by-word will generally produce mediocre results---different languages may express 
the same idea using different numbers of words, or words presented in different orders. This motivates 
architectures with encoder-decoder structure: the input sequence is first *encoded* into 
a sequence of latent vectors $h_1, \dots, h_N$ (via an RNN), which is then summarized by some fixed-length
context vector $c$ that somehow captures the meaning of the input text. The context $c$
is then fed to the *decoder*, which produces $y_1, \dots, y_M$. This is done sequentially
via another RNN. At each step, the model outputs $y_m$, in addition to a latent vector
$s_m$ that is fed into the next step. The hidden states are produced sequentially as
$s_m = f(s_{m-1}, y_{m-1}, c)$, with the corresponding outputs given by $y_m = g(y_{m-1}, s_m, c)$.
The context $c$ is present and constant throughout this whole procedure. 
The issue here is that $c$ acts as a bottleneck. As the length $N$ of the input sequence grows,
$c$ must somehow encode all of the meaning within a fixed-length vector. Ideally, we would like 
the context to adapt to the input length.

# Attention in RNNs
The now famous attention mechanism is originally proposed in @AlignAndTranslate as a solution to this
problem. They update the decoder architecture as 
$$
\begin{align}
&y_m = g(y_{m-1}, s_m, c_m), &&s_m = f(s_{m-1}, y_{m-1}, c_m),
\end{align}
$$

such that each output decoding step depends on a different context vector $c_m$.
We want the neural network to learn $c_m$ such that it provides a summary of the input sequence
that is particuarly useful for predicting the current output $y_m$. In machine translation,
we would generally expect input tokens that are approximately in the same location as
$y_m$ in the sequence to provide the most useful context. But it is possible that longer-range
dependencies are also important in translating the current word. The simple parameterization
$$
\begin{align}
&c_m = \sum_{n=1}^{N} a_{mn} h_n, &&\sum_{n=1}^{N} a_{mn} = 1
\end{align}
$$

gives the neural network this flexibility. The $a_{mn}$ are learnable parameters, so the network
can determine how to weight the $h_n$ (recall that these are the encoded inputs) based on the 
information available at the current step. Put more succinctly by the original authors:
"The decoder decides parts of the source sentence to pay attention to" (@AlignAndTranslate).
